title: Quasiparticle GW for Superconductors: Toward a Unified Treatment of Electron-Phonon and Electron-Plasmon Couplings
authors: Catalin D. Spataru, Christopher Renskers, Elena R. Margine
abstract: Superconducting two-dimensional materials, and in particular few-layer graphene, offer an exciting platform for low-power electronics, yet the origin of their unconventional superconductivity remains an open question. Prevailing theories, primarily rooted in the Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) framework that assumes electron-phonon interactions are the main mechanism of superconductivity, struggle to account quantitatively for the observed phenomena. Recent studies point to a plasmonic pairing mechanism in graphene systems; however, disentangling the relative contributions of phonon- and plasmon-mediated pairing remains challenging due to the lack of a satisfactory first-principles framework capable of accurately capturing dynamical screening effects in the electronic channel. Here, we present a new theoretical framework that extends the quasiparticle self-consistent GW method to the superconducting phase by coupling it with the Eliashberg treatment of both phonon- and plasmon-mediated interactions. Our approach, termed "s-qpGW", is on par with the state-of-the-art Eliashberg theory of superconductivity when applied to bulk metals, and correctly predicts the absence of superconductivity in doped monolayer graphene. To differentiate s-qpGW from conventional Eliashberg approaches, we study a simple model system, graphene with an artificially enhanced density of states, and demonstrate that s-qpGW captures dynamical Coulomb screening effects in ways that standard BCS theory cannot.
summary: The authors present a new theoretical framework, s-qpGW, that allows for a unified treatment of electron-phonon and electron-plasmon couplings in superconductors. This method fixes the issue of 'spurious' superconductivity predictions in 2D materials found in previous GW approaches. It provides a parameter-free way to study how dynamical screening by plasmons influences the superconducting state.
main_problem: The difficulty in accurately disentangling phonon-mediated and plasmon-mediated pairing mechanisms in 2D superconductors, and the failure of standard GW methods to avoid spurious superconductivity predictions.
main_result: The development of the s-qpGW framework, which correctly predicts the absence of superconductivity in doped monolayer graphene and provides a reliable way to capture dynamical Coulomb screening effects.
method: An extension of the quasiparticle self-consistent GW (s-qpGW) method coupled with Eliashberg theory, using a QP-projection to suppress spurious pairing from high-energy plasmons.
model_or_system: The study focuses on 2D materials (specifically doped monolayer and few-layer graphene, graphene superlattes, and rhombohedral trilayer graphene) and 3D bulk metals (Niobium).
key_observables: Superconducting transition temperature (Tc), superconducting gap (Delta), anomalous self-energy (phi), and mass renormalization function (Z).
important_parameters: Electron-phonon coupling strength (lambda), plasmon energy (omega_pl), Fermi energy (Ef), and the Coulomb pseudopotential (mu*).
main_assumptions: Uses the Migdal approximation for electron-phonon coupling, the GW approximation for self-energy, and neglects vertex corrections and the incoherent part of the spectral weight.
figures_summary: Figures show the convergence of the superconducting gap in Nb with respect to Matsubara frequency cutoffs and the calculated superconducting gap in a 2D model system with enhanced density of states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem of pairing mechanisms in 2D, presents the new s-qpGW theoretical framework, benchmarks the method against bulk Nb and graphene, demonstrates the capture of dynamical screening, and discusses computational implementation via a plasmon-pole model.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Calculated isotropic Eliashberg spectral function α2F (black line), and cumulative contri- bution to the electron-phonon coupling strength λ (red line) for: a) Doped monolayer graphene, and b) bulk Nb.

FIG. 1. a) Real part of the inverse dielectric function of doped graphene for momentum q = 10−8 m−1. b) Real part of the inverse dielectric function of bulk Nb for momentum q = 10−9 m−1. The inset in each panel shows an enlarged view near ω = 0, where ℜϵ−1(ω) changes from positive to neg- ative.

FIG. 3. a) Superconducting properties of doped monolayer graphene calculated at T = 3 K within s-GW, as a function of the energy cutoff along the Matsubara imaginary-frequency axis. b) Anoma- lous (pairing) self-energy and spectral function (solid red line) for an electron with energy EQP nk ≈ 0.1 eV above the Fermi level in doped mono- layer graphene calculated within the s-GW at T = 3 K. Solid green and blue lines represent the phonon and Coulomb contributions to the anoma- lous self-energy. The dotted black horizontal line is the static-screening value ϕC,stat, obtained using Eq. (23) with all right-hand-side quantities taken from the converged s-GW solution.

FIG. 4. Superconducting properties of bulk Nb cal- culated within s-GW (black squares), static Eliash- berg (green circles), and s-qpGW (red diamonds). a) The superconducting gap parameter calculated at T = 10 K, as function of the energy cutoff along the Matsubara imaginary-frequency axis. b) The converged superconducting gap as function of tem- perature.

FIG. 5. Anomalous (pairing) self-energy and spec- tral function (solid red line) for an electron with energy EQP nk ≈3 meV above the Fermi level in bulk Nb, calculated within the s-qpGW at T = 10 K. Solid green and blue lines represent the phonon and Coulomb contributions to the anoma- lous self-energy. The dotted black horizontal line is the static-screening value ϕC,stat, obtained using Eq. (23) with all right-hand-side quantities taken from the converged s-qpGW solution.
